frameworks.name: drift-diffusion
frameworks.one_line: Evidence accumulation extended from choice/RT/confidence to prospective difficulty judgments.

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: Difficulty defined as |pblue − 0.5| over stochastic color coherence.

frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: Confidence-comparison observer rejected versus absolute-accumulated-evidence comparison.

core_question: How do observers prospectively judge which of two perceptual stimuli will be easier to decide about, without completing either decision?
key_assumptions: Difficulty is defined by stimulus coherence distance from chance.
Observers can compare ongoing evidence streams across two stimuli in parallel.
A common bound terminates the difficulty judgment when accumulators diverge.
manipulations: Stimulus coherence pblue varied trial-to-trial across two patches.
Patch pairings spanning equal vs. unequal difficulty.
Condition where the dominant color of each stimulus was disclosed.
key_findings: Accuracy of difficulty judgment improved with the difference in stimulus difficulties.
Reaction times depended on individual stimulus difficulty, not solely on the difficulty difference.
When dominant colors were known, RTs depended only on the difficulty difference, as predicted.
An absolute-accumulated-evidence comparison model outperformed a confidence-comparison model.
interpretations: Prospective difficulty judgments arise from comparing absolute accumulated evidence between stimuli, terminating at a fixed gap.
Evidence accumulation accounts extend naturally from choice/RT/confidence to prospective metacognitive judgments of difficulty.
